messages.content: 2.	Read the story of Christmas in either: Matthew 1:18-25, 2:1-12 OR Luke 1:26-38, 2:1- in your own words

In the story of Christmas, as told in the Gospel of Luke, an angel named Gabriel was sent by God to a young woman named Mary, who lived in a town called Nazareth. The angel appeared to Mary and greeted her, telling her that she was highly favored and that God was with her.

Mary was initially perplexed and afraid, but the angel reassured her and told her not to be afraid, for she had found favor with God. The angel then shared the incredible news that Mary would conceive and give birth to a son, whom she should name Jesus. This child would be great and would be called the Son of the Most High, who would reign over the kingdom of David forever.

Mary questioned how this could be possible since she was not married and did not know a man. The angel explained that the Holy Spirit would come upon her, and the power of the Most High would overshadow her, enabling her to conceive miraculously. The angel further revealed that even Mary's relative, Elizabeth, who was considered barren, was also expecting a child in her old age.

Mary humbly accepted this news, expressing her willingness to serve the Lord and do His will. She acknowledged herself as the Lord's servant and trusted in God's plan for her life. The angel departed, leaving Mary to ponder this extraordinary event.

In the days that followed, Mary traveled with her betrothed, Joseph, to Bethlehem as part of a census ordered by the Roman emperor. While in Bethlehem, Mary went into labor, and she gave birth to her son, wrapping Him in swaddling clothes and laying Him in a manger since there was no room for them in the inn.

In the same region, there were shepherds looking after their sheep in the fields at night when suddenly an angel of the Lord appeared to them. The shepherds were frightened, but the angel reassured them, announcing the joyful news of the Savior's birth. The angel directed them to find the baby wrapped in swaddling clothes, lying in a manger.

Suddenly, a multitude of heavenly hosts joined the angel, praising God and proclaiming peace on earth. The shepherds were filled with awe and decided to go and see the child for themselves. They hurried to Bethlehem and found Mary, Joseph, and the baby, just as the angel had described. The shepherds shared their incredible encounter with others, and all who heard were amazed.

Mary treasured all these events in her heart, marveling at the prophecies fulfilled and the miraculous circumstances surrounding the birth of her son, Jesus.
simplyfy this to 100 words

In the story of Christmas, an angel named Gabriel appeared to Mary to deliver incredible news. He told her she would conceive and give birth to a son named Jesus, who would be the Son of the Most High. Mary questioned how this could be possible, but the angel explained it would happen through the Holy Spirit. Mary accepted this and later traveled to Bethlehem with Joseph, where she gave birth to Jesus in a manger. Shepherds were visited by angels who shared the news of Jesus' birth, and they hurried to see the baby. Mary treasured these events in her heart.

He told her she would conceive and give birth to a son named Jesus, who would be the Son of the Most High. Mary questioned how this could be possible, but the angel explained it would happen through the Holy Spirit. Mary accepted this and later traveled to Bethlehem with Joseph, where she gave birth to Jesus in a manger. Shepherds were visited by angels who shared the news of Jesus’ birth, and they hurried to see the baby. Mary treasured these events in her heart.

An angel named Gabriel visited Mary and told her she would have a special baby named Jesus. Mary was unsure how this could happen, but the angel said God would make it possible. Mary and Joseph traveled to Bethlehem, where Jesus was born in a manger because there was no room in the inn. Shepherds nearby saw angels who told them about Jesus, so they went to see him. Mary remembered all these amazing things in her heart.

Nearby, shepherds saw angels who told them about Jesus, so they came to see him. Mary held these special memories in her heart.

An angel named Gabriel visited Mary and told her she would have a baby named Jesus. They went to Bethlehem, and Jesus was born in a manger. Shepherds saw angels who told them about Jesus, so they came to see him. Mary treasured these memories.

Gabriel told Mary she would have a baby named Jesus. They went to Bethlehem and Jesus was born in a manger. Shepherds saw angels who told them about Jesus, so they came to see him. Mary cherished these memories.

3.	Research the origins of the following symbols of Christmas and record your findings.  ¨ What does Advent mean?  
¨ The Christmas Tree ¨ Gift giving 
¨ Christmas stockings ¨ Saint Nicholas 
¨ Christmas carols 
¨ The Nativity ¨ What does the word ‘Christmas’ mean?

¨ Advent: Advent is a term derived from the Latin word "adventus," meaning "coming" or "arrival." It refers to the period of preparation and anticipation observed by Christians in the weeks leading up to Christmas. It usually begins on the fourth Sunday before Christmas and lasts until Christmas Eve. Advent is a time of spiritual reflection and expectation, commemorating the coming of Jesus Christ into the world.

¨ The Christmas Tree: The tradition of decorating a Christmas tree has pagan origins, dating back to ancient times when evergreen trees symbolized life and renewal during the winter solstice. The practice was later adopted by Christians as a part of their Christmas celebrations, representing eternal life through Jesus. Martin Luther is credited with popularizing the candle-lit Christmas tree in the 16th century.

¨ Gift Giving: The tradition of giving gifts during Christmas has its roots in the biblical story of the three wise men who brought gifts to the baby Jesus. It also reflects the act of giving that God displayed by giving his son, Jesus, to the world. The practice of gift-giving has evolved over time and is now a way to demonstrate love, generosity, and share joy during the holiday season.

¨ Christmas Stockings: The tradition of hanging stockings originated from the story of Saint Nicholas (also known as Santa Claus). According to legend, Saint Nicholas helped a poor family by secretly leaving gold coins in their stockings, which were drying near the fireplace. This practice grew, and stockings became a symbol of good fortune and receiving gifts during Christmas.

¨ Saint Nicholas: Saint Nicholas was a Christian bishop living in the 4th century in what is now modern-day Turkey. He was known for his generosity and kindness, especially towards children and the needy. Over time, the stories of Saint Nicholas evolved and merged with various cultural traditions, ultimately leading to the figure of Santa Claus as we know him today.

¨ Christmas Carols: Christmas carols are songs specifically associated with the holiday season and the birth of Jesus Christ. The tradition of singing carols during Christmas originated in medieval Europe when singers would go from house to house singing hymns and spreading joy during the festive season. Carols started to become more formally organized and widely performed in the 19th century.

¨ The Nativity: The Nativity refers to the birth of Jesus Christ, as described in the Bible. It is the central event of the Christmas story and holds significant importance for Christians. The Nativity scene, often depicted with figurines or actors portraying characters such as Mary, Joseph, baby Jesus, shepherds, and the three wise men, commemorates and visually represents the biblical account of Jesus' birth.

¨ Christmas: The word "Christmas" originates from the Old English term "Cristesmaesse," which means "Christ's Mass." It refers to the Christian celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Combining the words "Christ" (referring to Jesus) and "Mass" (a church service or celebration), Christmas represents the observance of the birth of Jesus through religious ceremonies, traditions, and festivities.

According to legend, Saint Nicholas helped a poor family by secretly leaving gold coins in their stockings, which were drying near the fireplace. This practice grew, and stockings became a symbol of good fortune and receiving gifts during Christmas.

¨ Saint Nicholas: Saint Nicholas was a Christian bishop living in the 4th century in what is now modern-day Turkey. ¨ Christmas Carols: Christmas carols are songs specifically associated with the holiday season and the birth of Jesus Christ. The tradition of singing carols during Christmas originated in medieval Europe when singers would go from house to house singing hymns and spreading joy during the festive season. Carols started to become more formally organized and widely performed in the 19th century.

¨ The Nativity: The Nativity refers to the birth of Jesus Christ, as described in the Bible. It is the central event of the Christmas story and holds significant importance for Christians. The Nativity scene, often depicted with figurines or actors portraying characters such as Mary, Joseph, baby Jesus, shepherds, and the three wise men, commemorates and visually represents the biblical account of Jesus’ birth.

¨ Christmas: The word “Christmas” originates from the Old English term “Cristesmaesse,” which means “Christ’s Mass.” It refers to the Christian celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Combining the words “Christ” (referring to Jesus) and “Mass” (a church service or celebration), Christmas represents the observance of the birth of Jesus through religious ceremonies, traditions, and festivities.

¨ Advent: Advent is the time leading up to Christmas when Christians prepare for and anticipate the arrival of Jesus. It starts on the fourth Sunday before Christmas and lasts until Christmas Eve. It's a period of reflection and expectation.

¨ The Christmas Tree: Decorating a Christmas tree comes from ancient times when evergreen trees symbolized life during the winter solstice. Christians adopted it to represent eternal life through Jesus. Martin Luther popularized the candle-lit Christmas tree.

¨ Gift Giving: Giving gifts at Christmas originated from the story of the three wise men who brought gifts to baby Jesus. It reflects God's act of giving His son to the world. Gift-giving is a way to show love, generosity, and share joy.

¨ Christmas Stockings: Hanging stockings comes from the story of Saint Nicholas, who secretly left gold coins in stockings drying near the fireplace. Stockings became a symbol of good fortune and receiving gifts at Christmas.

¨ Saint Nicholas: Saint Nicholas was a generous Christian bishop in the 4th century. Over time, his stories merged with cultural traditions, eventually leading to the figure of Santa Claus.

¨ Christmas Carols: Christmas carols are songs about Jesus' birth. Singing carols originated in medieval Europe when singers spread joy by going from house to house. Carols became more organized and popular in the 19th century.

¨ The Nativity: The Nativity refers to Jesus' birth as described in the Bible. It's a central event in the Christmas story, often represented with figures of Mary, Joseph, baby Jesus, shepherds, and the wise men.

¨ Christmas: The word "Christmas" comes from "Christ's Mass," the Christian celebration of Jesus' birth. It combines "Christ" (referring to Jesus) and "Mass" (a church service or celebration). Christmas includes religious ceremonies, traditions, and festivities.
